rationale: The citing paper references Khan and Dhar (2006) in its theoretical contribution section as a specific alternative explanation, labeling it a product-type licensing effect and explicitly distinguishing its own motivation-balancing account from that mechanism. It argues that its observed motivation shifts arise with unplanned purchase consideration and are not just hedonic-versus-utilitarian sequencing, which is a direct conceptual qualification of the licensing account's scope in this context. The paper also uses the citation in managerial discussion when contrasting its recommended messaging strategy with sequential hedonic/utilitarian alternation.
relation_description: The citing paper uses the cited licensing-effect work as a nearby theoretical account and then differentiates its own mechanism from it. In doing so, it narrows and qualifies how licensing-style product-type alternation explains in-store sequential choices.
